reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest voice prioritizes workload feasibility, professional autonomy in classroom assessment, and staffing sustainability. The interest is defensible under equity framing only if implementation does not hollow out professional judgment or create compliance-audit burden.
reactions.interest: Teachers' operational capacity, professional autonomy in selection and assessment, workload sustainability, and the viability of staffing differentiated instruction across intake cohorts.
reactions.public_good_frame: Educational equity is legitimate, but cannot be achieved by transferring administrative burden and compliance risk to teachers, or by designing assessments that devalue subject expertise.
reactions.argument: S1 redesigns the admission gate but does not solve the downstream sorting problem: if the intake is rebalanced, the teaching cohort becomes more heterogeneous in prior attainment while the number of teachers per class and their subject-expertise distribution remain unchanged. The 'less preparation-sensitive assessment' is a signal to pedagogues that the *definition* of selection is shifting from test-coachability to something else—but the scenario does not specify what. If it means 'portfolio assessment' or 'teacher-observed readiness,' this creates new professional-autonomy risks: teachers are now gatekeepers of an equity-sensitive selection process, with compliance audits to follow. If non-state maintainers are brought under 'uniform equity-based admission rules' via subsidy conditionality, the Ministry is imposing selection definitions on schools with different pedagogical models and institutional autonomy claims, and teachers in those schools will absorb the implementation cost. The 'medium' one-off cost for assessment redesign masks a recurring compliance burden on schools to administer a new instrument, validate it locally, and document fairness. The 'compliance-audit mechanism' flagged in implementation_steps does not say who staffs it: if schools are expected to support auditors, this is uncompensated workload. Support for S1 is conditional on three conditions: (1) the new assessment is standardized and centrally administered, not delegated to school-level judgment or portfolio review; (2) intake rebalancing does not increase class sizes or reduce per-pupil staffing in any cohort, and does not create mixed-attainment classes without additional staffing or time for lesson planning; (3) non-state maintainers are brought under uniform rules *without* imposing pedagogical methods or assessment definitions that contradict their institutional autonomy or teaching model.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support if: (a) the new assessment is standardized centrally and does not require teacher-level gatekeeping or compliance audits; (b) intake rebalancing is explicitly decoupled from class-size or staffing increases, and mixed-attainment cohorts are resourced with additional pedagogical time or support staff; (c) subsidy-conditional rules on non-state schools are narrow (admission quota format and SES indicator only) and do not mandate pedagogical methods. Would oppose if: (a) the new assessment is portfolio-based or teacher-observed-readiness, creating new compliance and professional-autonomy risk; (b) rebalanced intake is absorbed into existing class sizes and staffing without change; (c) subsidy conditionality extends to teaching methods or curriculum.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest voice prioritizes staffing feasibility, workload predictability, and pedagogical stability. The interest is defensible under equity framing only if caps do not create a decade-long transition burden or recruit teachers into two incompatible pedagogical regimes.
reactions.interest: Teachers' staffing stability, pedagogical predictability during a long transition, avoiding dual-regime teaching in the same school for a decade, and avoiding 'advanced-programme' expectations imposed without additional preparation time or resources.
reactions.public_good_frame: Gradual structural change can be equitable, but only if the transition does not impose ad-hoc pedagogical demands on teachers or destabilize the schools undergoing it.
reactions.argument: S2 caps early-entry places on a multi-year trajectory while felmenő rendszer guarantees protect already-enrolled cohorts—this spreads change 'over a decade.' But the implementation_steps assume this is painless: a teacher in a general school during years 2-9 of the phase-down must teach mixed cohorts where some pupils entered the escola under the old rules (with selective peers and selective curriculum prior experience) and others did not. The 'advanced-programme funding' is intended to 'retain ambitious families in the common track,' but this is not the same as funding *additional pedagogy*: it often means additional hours or enrichment that competes for planning time or requires teachers to prepare differentiated materials without compensatory time. The scenario's own uncertainties flag that 'general schools can absorb and challenge high-achievers' is an assumption, not established fact—yet the implementation plan assumes it will happen if funded. A teacher in a general school has no obligation to believe that 'advanced programming' will succeed if subject expertise, class size, and time are unchanged. The 'medium' recurring cost for 'parallel-regime administration' during the transition is vague: if it means schools must keep two administrative tracks (old-rules and new-rules cohorts), this is a compliance and timetabling burden on school leadership, not teachers directly. But if 'advanced-programme funding' flows with string-attached accountability (progress metrics, outcome audits), then teachers absorb the reporting load. Support is conditional on three conditions: (1) 'advanced-programme funding' is not a euphemism for additional unpaid curriculum design or enrichment, and comes with protected planning time; (2) mixed-cohort teaching in transition years does not increase class sizes or reduce staffing; (3) the phase-down is legislatively anchored with an entrenchment mechanism (multi-year notice, supermajority reversal) so that teachers are not caught mid-transition by a reversal that leaves them staffing both old and new curricula simultaneously.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support if: (a) 'advanced-programme funding' comes with explicit planning-time allocation and is not contingent on teacher-designed enrichment or additional assessment; (b) mixed-cohort teaching in transition years is not absorbed into existing class sizes, and schools receive additional staffing or time during the overlap period; (c) legislative entrenchment is actual (e.g. supermajority, sunset-reversal, or cross-party pact) and is publicly visible, so teachers can plan careers with confidence that the trajectory will not reverse mid-course. Would oppose if: (a) 'advanced programming' is assumed to be delivered within existing time and staffing; (b) a successor government reverses the cap without entrenchment friction, leaving schools mid-transition; (c) the transition is extended beyond 10 years or cohorts are exempted in ways that create perpetual dual-regime teaching.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest voice prioritizes staffing adequacy, subject expertise alignment, and pedagogical stability. S3 is structurally inimical to all three: it postpones selection to 14 while increasing heterogeneity within classrooms, and relies on 'teacher-quality and diagnostic-support investment' that the scenario's own implementation_planning flags as a 'binding constraint' (acute shortage).
reactions.interest: Teachers' ability to teach to the content standard and to the pupils in front of them without pedagogical overload; staffing stability and subject-expertise matching; avoiding a decade of retraining that makes the teacher labor market unstable; and career continuity for mid-career teachers.
reactions.public_good_frame: Structural equity reform is legitimate, but cannot be implemented on a foundation of unresolved teacher shortage. Trading selection sorting for classroom heterogeneity without solving the shortage converts an intake problem into a delivery problem that lands entirely on teachers.
reactions.argument: S3 is the 'comprehensive school to age 14' design. It postpones selection to 14, which requires heterogeneous classrooms from ages 10-14 where prior attainment ranges from 1-2 years below grade level to 1-2 years above, without between-school separation. The mechanism depends on 'teacher-quality and diagnostic-support investment' bundled with delayed selection. But the scenario's own implementation_planning section states: 'teacher supply, especially in maths and science, can support de-tracked heterogeneous classrooms [assumption]' and the uncertainties section lists 'whether teacher supply can sustain heterogeneous classrooms [confidence: low].' This is not a minor uncertainty: it is the load-bearing assumption. A teacher in a maths classroom with 8 pupils at two years below grade level, 12 at grade level, and 5 at two years above, with class size 25, has one of three options: (1) teach to the middle, leaving both groups undersupported; (2) differentiate, which requires preparation of three parallel tracks of material and assessment, without compensatory planning time; (3) teach to the weakest, accepting that the capable pupils are unchallenged and that middle-class families exit the school. The scenario's political_risks section identifies exactly option (3) as the core problem: 'Middle-class exit to non-state or private schools... [that exit] does not harm the exiting families; it concentrates the downside on the low-SES pupils left behind, so within-disadvantaged distributional harm is possible even if the aggregate gradient narrows.' That is a teacher-interest problem masquerading as a politics problem. If middle-class exit occurs (and it is the most likely outcome under conditions of teacher shortage and large classroom heterogeneity), then the teacher is left teaching even more homogeneously disadvantaged cohorts with even less subject expertise support and no relief from preparation burden. The scenario asks teachers to solve a structural problem (sorting) by absorbing unprecedented heterogeneity, with a retraining programme ('teacher differentiation retraining') that is not resourced with a per-teacher cost, timeline per person, or coverage probability. The implementation_steps say 'sequence teacher differentiation retraining before, then during, the pilot rather than concurrently,' which means years 1-4 are a retraining phase. During that phase, are cohorts in pilot schools taught by retraining teachers (which degrades quality), or are pilot schools staffed by volunteers with excess capacity (which does not exist in shortage conditions)? The scenario does not say. The political_risks section names 'Teacher shortage makes de-tracked delivery degrade academic quality, feeding the flight risk' as a risk, but this is not a risk—it is a prediction. Given unresolved teacher shortage and the scenario's own confidence-low assessment of supply adequacy, degraded delivery is near-certain, not a risk to be managed. For these reasons, this voice opposes S3 categorically, not conditionally.
reactions.condition_to_change: Conditional support is not credible because the core assumption is that teacher supply is adequate, and the scenario's own planning section calls it a 'binding constraint' and the uncertainties section rates confidence as 'low.' The condition to change this stance would require: (a) a separate teacher-supply reform that achieves +15% hiring and retraining capacity in shortage subjects within years 1-4, with staffing guarantees for pilot schools, AND (b) a protected funding line for diagnostic-support systems (the 'Finnish-style bundle') that is explicitly ring-fenced against fiscal consolidation, with 5-year forward commitments to schools. Without both conditions, the reform trades intake selection for classroom heterogeneity under conditions of teacher shortage, which converts an equity access problem into a delivery quality problem that lands on teachers and harms the most-disadvantaged pupils. That exchange is not supportable.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest voice prioritizes workload predictability, stability of professional context, and autonomy from external compliance-audit pressure. S4 leaves the selection structure intact but adds 'rigorous curriculum standards' and targeted support for disadvantaged schools. The interest is defensible if standards do not translate into compliance audits on teacher performance, and if targeted support reaches schools as resources, not requirements.
reactions.interest: Teachers in disadvantaged schools: freedom from stigmatizing 'support' packages, avoidance of standards-based performance audits, and avoidance of role expansion (remediation, social support, etc.) without compensatory resources. Teachers in selective schools: stability of status and pedagogical autonomy.
reactions.public_good_frame: Non-structural compensation is legitimate, but only if it is resource-additive, not audit-intensive, and does not stigmatize disadvantaged schools or create two-tier teacher professionalism.
reactions.argument: S4 leaves the structure intact (6/8-year gymnázium entry unchanged) and proposes to 'neutralize equity effects' through targeted funding, rigorous standards, grade-retention reduction, and school-clustering administration. The teacher-interest problem is that 'rigorous national curriculum standards' is ambiguous. If it means 'a defensible, challenging curriculum that all schools follow' (a good), then it is neutral to teaching. If it means 'standards-based accountability with teacher-level performance audits,' then it is a workload and compliance burden, especially in disadvantaged schools where outcome metrics will be sensitive to demography and prior attainment, not instructional effort. The scenario's own political_risks section warns: 'Budget vulnerability: compensation lines are the first cut in fiscal consolidation, so gains are reversible across fiscal cycles.' This directly harms teachers in disadvantaged schools. A teacher hired on the promise of targeted support and teacher premia ($^{—}$ implementation_steps: 'teacher premia for disadvantaged schools') faces the risk that 5 years into the programme, a fiscal consolidation cuts the premium, leaving the teacher in a school that is now known as a 'disadvantaged-support' school without the compensation. This creates a two-tier labor market where teaching in disadvantaged schools becomes a high-risk career choice. The scenario frames this as a 'political risk' (reversibility across fiscal cycles), but it is a direct teacher-interest problem. The 'TEIP-style package' reference is to the Portuguese case, where targeted funding supported schools in consolidating networks and improving management. But Portugal did not combine this with early selection (ages 10-12) at Hungary's intensity. A disadvantaged-school teacher in Hungary inherits the problem of teaching pupils who were not selected into gimnázium, which in Portugal's case (with selection at 15, not 10-12) was less structurally consequential. The comparison is weak. Support for S4 is conditional on: (1) 'rigorous standards' does not translate into performance audits or value-added metrics tied to teacher evaluation; (2) 'teacher premia' are legislatively protected (not just Ministry commitments) and survive fiscal consolidation; (3) targeted funding is formula-based and reaches schools as block grants, not as tied grants with compliance conditions. If any of these conditions fail, S4 is workload-additive and status-reducing for disadvantaged-school teachers without the equity gain of structural reform.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support if: (a) 'rigorous national curriculum standards' are decoupled from teacher-level accountability or performance audits; instead, standards are published as guidance, and schools are audited on curriculum coverage and resource adequacy, not on outcomes; (b) 'teacher premia' are written into the Nkt. (not as Ministry administrative rule) and are explicitly protected from fiscal consolidation by a supermajority-to-cut rule or by ring-fencing in the education budget; (c) targeted funding reaches schools as block grants with minimal compliance reporting, and the diagnosis and remediation model is school-led, not imposed. Would oppose if: (a) 'rigorous standards' is paired with value-added or standardized-test accountability systems that hold teachers responsible for pupil outcomes; (b) 'teacher premia' are administrative policy (reversible in the next budget cycle), not legislative; (c) targeted 'support' packages come with implementation conditions, audit requirements, or role expansion (diagnostic assessment, remediation, attendance-support) that increase teacher workload without compensatory time or staffing.
